metadata.name: Filing Status Threshold Matrix
metadata.description: Consolidated filing-status-dependent thresholds for all five IRS filing statuses. Single lookup point for threshold-dependent calculations — federal tax, Social Security taxation, IRMAA, IRA eligibility, and SALT caps all keyed by filing status.
metadata.update_frequency: annual (January, after IRS Rev. Proc. published)
sources: SSA (SS taxation provisional income thresholds — unchanged since 1993)
26 U.S.C. § 1(a)-(d) (tax rate tables by filing status)
Tax Foundation historical bracket data (CRS Report RL34498)
notes: Five IRS filing statuses: single, married_filing_jointly (MFJ), married_filing_separately (MFS), head_of_household (HoH), qualifying_surviving_spouse (QSS).
QSS uses MFJ rates and thresholds for up to 2 tax years after spouse's death, if dependent child.
MFS is the most restrictive status — collapses Roth IRA access, SS taxation thresholds, and IRMAA thresholds.
HoH requires maintaining a home for a qualifying dependent. Brackets are wider than single, narrower than MFJ.

Cross-references: federal-tax-brackets.json (historical brackets), rates-annual.json (current-year constants), ira-limits.json (Roth phase-outs), obbba-tax-provisions.json (senior deduction), medicare-rates.json (IRMAA).

Historical data covers 2016-2025. Pre-TCJA years (2016-2017) use the 7-rate structure (10/15/25/28/33/35/39.6%). TCJA years (2018-2025) use the 7-rate structure (10/12/22/24/32/35/37%). OBBBA senior deduction and expanded SALT caps only apply starting 2025.

corrections.description: Fixed 2026 HoH tax brackets and MFS 35%/37% bracket boundaries against IRS Rev. Proc. 2025-32 Tables 2 and 4. HoH brackets 10%/24%/32% had been incorrectly derived from Single brackets; MFS 35% max was 384225 instead of 384350 (half of MFJ 768700). Also corrected MFJ/QSS 35% max (768450->768700) and 37% min (768451->768701).
Fixed 2026 HoH bracket thresholds: 10% max 17650->17700, 24% max 201775->201750, 32% range 201776-256225->201751-256200, per IRS Rev. Proc. 2025-32 Table 2

known_issues.issue: HoH brackets from 22% and above may be incorrectly derived from Single bracket boundaries. HoH 10% bracket max values are systematically 1 less than IRS-published values. Requires per-year Rev. Proc. verification.

single.description: Unmarried, or legally separated/divorced with no qualifying dependents for HoH
single.eligibility: Default for unmarried individuals not qualifying for HoH

social_security_taxation._note: Combined income = AGI + tax-exempt interest + 50% of SS benefits. Thresholds unchanged since 1993 — not indexed.

roth_ira._note: Linear phase-out between thresholds. Rounded down to nearest $10.

traditional_ira_deductibility._note: If person is active participant in employer plan (TSP counts). Non-active-participant has no income limit.

married_filing_jointly.description: Married couple filing a joint return, or QSS (qualifying surviving spouse) during the 2-year bridge
married_filing_jointly.eligibility: Married as of Dec 31 of tax year. QSS: up to 2 years after spouse's death if dependent child.

social_security_taxation._note: NOT double the single thresholds. This asymmetry creates the marriage penalty for SS taxation.

spousal_ira.requirement: Non-working spouse can contribute using working spouse's earned income
spousal_ira._note: Spousal IRA uses same MFJ MAGI thresholds. Both spouses can contribute $7,500 each ($8,600 each if 50+).

traditional_ira_deductibility._note: If NEITHER spouse is active participant, no income limit for deductibility.

married_filing_separately.description: Married couple electing to file separate returns. Most restrictive status for retirement planning.
married_filing_separately.eligibility: Married as of Dec 31 of tax year, electing separate filing.
married_filing_separately._warning: MFS collapses most benefit thresholds. Roth IRA effectively unavailable. SS fully taxable. IRMAA uses single-equivalent thresholds with accelerated surcharges. Use only when strategic (medical deductions, income-driven repayment, IRMAA management).

tax._bracket_note: MFS brackets are exactly half of MFJ through the 35% bracket, then compressed at the 37% threshold.

social_security_taxation._note: MFS filers who lived with spouse at any time during the year: ALL Social Security benefits are potentially taxable from dollar one. The 50%/85% thresholds are effectively zero.

irmaa._note: MFS has only 3 IRMAA tiers. Jumps directly from tier 0 to tier 4 — skipping 3 intermediate tiers. This is the most punitive IRMAA schedule.

roth_ira._note: If lived with spouse at any time during year, phase-out is $0 to $10,000. Effectively zero Roth IRA access. If lived apart ALL year, single limits apply instead.

traditional_ira_deductibility._note: Same $0-$10,000 restriction as Roth IRA if lived with spouse.

salt_deduction_cap._note: MFS SALT cap is half of MFJ cap.

head_of_household.description: Unmarried person who pays more than half the cost of maintaining a home for a qualifying dependent.
head_of_household.eligibility: Must be unmarried (or considered unmarried) on Dec 31. Must pay >50% of home costs. Qualifying dependent must live in home >50% of year.
qualifying_dependents: Qualifying child (son, daughter, stepchild, foster child, sibling, or descendant of any of these)
Qualifying relative who is a dependent and lived in the home all year (parent exception: parent need not live with you)

obbba_senior_deduction._note: HoH phase-out uses single threshold, not MFJ.

tax._bracket_note: 10% and 12% brackets are wider than single. 22% and above match single thresholds.

irmaa._note: HoH uses the same IRMAA tiers as single filers.

traditional_ira_deductibility._note: Same as single if active participant in employer plan.

salt_deduction_cap._note: Same SALT cap as single filers.

qualifying_surviving_spouse.description: Surviving spouse for up to 2 tax years after year of spouse's death, if maintaining home for dependent child.
qualifying_surviving_spouse.eligibility: Must not have remarried. Must have a dependent child who lived in the home all year. Available only for the 2 tax years following the year of death.
qualifying_surviving_spouse._note: Uses MFJ rates and thresholds. This is the 2-year bridge before dropping to single or HoH.

roth_ira._note: Same as MFJ. Spousal IRA no longer applies (spouse deceased).

traditional_ira_deductibility._note: Same as MFJ for active participants.

transition_rules._description: State machine transitions for filing status determination. Each event changes the filing status for the current and/or subsequent tax years.

marriage.effective: Year of marriage (married as of Dec 31 = MFJ for full year)
marriage.notes: MFS is an annual election — couple can switch between MFJ and MFS each year.

divorce.effective: Year of final divorce decree (if finalized by Dec 31)

death_of_spouse._note: The QSS bridge provides 2 years of MFJ-equivalent treatment. Then status drops — this is the widow's tax cliff.

dependent_ages_out.trigger: Last qualifying dependent no longer meets dependency test (age 19, or 24 if student)
dependent_ages_out._note: Loss of last dependent can trigger HoH → single, losing $8,050 in standard deduction.

remarriage.notes: QSS status terminates upon remarriage. VA DIC/CHAMPVA remarriage rules are separate — see federal_veteran_benefits.

historical_years.source: IRS Rev. Proc. 2015; CMS IRMAA announcement

salt_deduction_cap._note: No federal SALT cap prior to TCJA (2018).
No federal SALT cap prior to TCJA (2018).

irmaa._note: MFS has compressed IRMAA schedule — see current year for details.
